title: 第 29 節
en: ANTERIORITY OF VEDANTA OK SAJfKHYA. 22Q important work, for we find similar short, yet old Sfttraworks, for instance, the Sarvanukrama and other Anukramanis described in my History of Ancient Sanskrit Literature1. However, in matters of this kind we must avoid being too positive either in denying or asserting the age and authenticity of Sanskrit texts. All I can say is that there is no mark of modern age in their language, though the commentary is, no doubt, of a later date. What weighs with me is the fact that Indian Pandits evidently considered the Tattva-sam&sa-Sutras as the original outlines of the Samkhya-philosophy, while the idea that they are a later spurious production rests, -as far as I can see at present, on no real argument whatever.
Anteriority of Ved&nta or Sftwiliya.

It must be clear from all this how useless it would be, with the limited means at our disposal, to attempt to prove the anteriority either of the Vedanta or of the S&mkhya, as systems of philosophy, and as distinguished from the Sutras in which we possess them. External or historical evidence we have none, and internal evidence, though it may support a suggestion, can but seldom amount to positive proof. We can understand how, out of the seeds scattered about in the Upanishads, there could arise in time the systematic arrangement and final representation of systems such as have been handed down to us in the Sutras of the Ved&nta, the Samkhya, and the other schools. It cannot be denied that in the Upanishad period Vedantic ideas are certainly more , prevalent than those of the Samkhya. I go even a step further and admit that the Samkhya-philosophy may have been a kind of toning down of the extreme Monism of the Advaita Ved&nta, I think we can enter into the misgivings and fears of those who felt startled by the unflinching Monism of *the Vedanta, at least as interpreted by the school which was represented rather than founded by $amkara. Now, the two points which are most likely to have caused difficulty 1 These Anufcramas have been very carefully published in the Anecdota Oxoniensia by Professor Macdonell, to whom I had handed over my materials.

or given offence to ordinary consciences, would seem to have been the total denial of what is meant by the reality of the objective world, and the required surrender of all individuality on the part of the subject, that is, of ourselves. These are the points which seem most startling even to ourselves, and it is quite possible that they may have given rise to another system free from these startling doctrines, such as we find in the Samkhya. They certainly formed the chief stumbling-block to R&manuga and those who had come before him, such as Bodhayana and other Purva&aryas, and led them to propound their own more human interpretation of the Vedanta, though sacrificing the Isvara in order to save the reality of each Purusha.

These conflicting views of the world, of the soul, and of God, emerge already in the Upanishads ; and in a few of them, the $vetasvataras Maitray., and Ka^a Upanishads, for instance, there are utterances that come very near to what we know as S&mkhya rather than Vedanta doctrines. Vedanta ideas preponderate, however, so decidedly in the Upanishad literature, that we can well understand that in the oral tradition of the schools the Samkhya doctrines should have exercised a limited influence only, whatever favour they may have found with those who were repelled by the extreme views of the monistic Vedanta. The followers of Kapila had an advantage over the Ved&ntists in admitting a Prakriti, or a something objective, independent of Brahman or Purusha, though called into life and activity by the look of Purusha only, and disappearing when that look ceased. They were also less opposed to the common consciousness of mankind in admitting the reality of individual souls. Dualism is always more popular than rigorous Monism, and the S&wkhya was clearly dualistic when it postulated nature, not only as the result of Avidya or Maya, but as something real in the ordinary sense of that word, and when it allowed to the individual souls or frivas also an independent character. It should be remembered that the denial of an Isvara or personal Lord did not probably form part of the original S&mkhya, as presented to us in the Tattva-sam&sa. . It would seem therefore that on, these very important points the S&mkhya was more ATHEISM AND ORTHODOXY. 23!

conciliatory and less defiant to the common sense of mankind than the Vedanta, and though this is far from proving that it was therefore posterior to the Vedanta in its severest form, it might well be accepted as an indication that these two streams of thought followed parallel courses, starting from a common fund of ancient Vedic thoughts, but diverging afterwards, the Vedanta unflinchingly following its straight course, the other, the Samkhya, avoiding certain whirlpools of thought which seemed dangerous to the ordinary swimmer. To the people at large it would naturally seem as if the Vedanta taught the oneness of all individual souls or subjects in Brahman, and the illusory character of all that is objective, while the Samkhya allowed at all events the temporary reality of the objective world and the multiplicity of individual souls. Of course, we must leave it an open question for the present whether the extreme monistic view of the Veda was due to $amkara, or whether, like Ramanugra, he also could claim the authority of Purva/c^ryas in his interpretation of Badaraya^a's Sutras. If that were so, the difference between the two systems would certainly seem to be irreconcilable, while minor differences between them would in India at least admit of a friendly adjustment.
Atheism and Orthodoxy.

Even on what seems to us so vital a point in every philosophy as theism or atheism, Indian philosophers seem to have been able to come to an understanding and a compromise. We must remember 'that in the eyes of the Brahinans the Samkhya is atheistic and yet orthodox. This seems to us impossible ; but the fact is that orthodoxy has a very different meaning in India from what it has with us. What we mean by orthodoxy was with them not much more than a recognition of the supreme authority of the Veda. The Samkhya, whatever we may think of its Vedic character, never denies the authority of the Veda in so many words, though it may express a less decided submission to it. Whether in its origin the Samkhya was quite independent of the Veda, is difficult to say. Some scholars think that the recognition of the supreme authority 232 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

of the Sruti was an afterthought with Kapila, a mere stroke of theological diplomacy. But if so, we should be forced to admit that the Samkhya philosophers wished, by means of this diplomacy, to be raised to the same position which others, such as the Vedantists, had occupied before them ; and so far it might seem to indicate the posteriority of the Samkhya, as a system of philosophy.

It is important here to remember that the Samkhya not only declared for the authority of the Veda, but had never openly rejected it, like Brihaspati or Buddha. It is quite another question whether it really carried out the spirit of the Veda, particularly of the Upanishads. That $amkara, the great defender of Vedantism, should deny the correctness of the interpretation of the Veda, adopted by Kapila, proves after all no more than that a difference of opinion existed between the two, but it would show at the same time that Kapila, as well as $amkara, had tried to represent his philosophy as supported by passages from the Veda. To judge from a passage in the beginning of the Samkhya-karikas it might seem indeed that Kapila placed his own philosophy above the Veda. But he really says no more there than that certain remedies for the removal of pain, enjoined by the Veda, are good, and that other remedies enjoined by philosophy are likewise good; but that of the two the latter are better, that is, more efficacious (Tattva Kaumudi, v. 2,). This does not affect the authority of the Veda as a whole, as compared with philosophy or human knowledge. We must not forget that after all it is Sruti or revelation itself which declares that all remedies are palliative only, and that real freedom (Moksha) from all suffering can be derived from philosophical knowledge only, and that this is incomparably higher than sacrifices or other meritorious acts (S&mkhya-prava&ana I, 5).
Authority of tlie Veda.

What authority Kapila assigns to the Veda may be gathered from what he says about the three possible sources of knowledge, perception, inference, and Aptavafeana, that is the received, correct, or true word, or, it may be, the word of a trustworthy person. He explains SAJ^KHYA HOSTILE TO PRIESTHOOD. 233 Aptavaifcana in v. 5 by Aptasruti, which, clearly means received revelation or revelation from a trustworthy source. However the commentators may differ, $ruti can here mean the Veda only, though, no doubt, the Veda as interpreted by Kapila. And that the Veda is not only considered as equal to sensuous perception and inference, but is placed by him on an even higher pedestal, is shown by the fact that Kapila (Sutras V, 51) declares it to be self-evident, Svatafeprama/uain, while perception and inference are not, but are admitted to be liable to error and to require confirmation.

Though it is true, therefore, that with the true Samkhya philosopher the Veda does not possess that superhuman authority which is ascribed to it by Badarayam, I cannot bring myself to believe that this concession on the part of Kapila was a mere artifice to escape the fate which, for instance, befell Buddha. There are many passages where Kapila appeals quite naturally to $ruti or revelation. In I, 36 he appeals to both /Sruti and Nyaya, reasoning, but in many places he appeals to /Sruti alone. That revelation is to be looked upon as superior to experience or sensuous perception is stated by him in so many words in I, 147, where we read ' There is no denial of what is established by jSiruti/ Again, when the Nyaya philosophy tries to establish by reasoning that the organs of sense are formed of the elements, Kapila squashes the whole argument by a simple appeal to $ruti. ' They cannot be so formed/ he says, ' because $ruti says that they are formed of Ahamkara, self-consciousness (II, 30) V Other passages where the authority of Sruti is invoked as paramount by Kapila, or supposed to be so by the commentator, may be found in Samkhya-Sutras I, 36 ; 77 ; 83 ; 147 ; 154; II, so ; 22 ; III, 15; 80 ; IV, 22 ; &c.
S£m3Lhya hostile to Priesthood.

There is one passage only in which a decidedly hostile feeling towards the Brahmanic priesthood may be discovered in Kapila's Sutras, and it seems full of meaning. Among the different kinds of bondage to which men are 1 But are not the elements mere Viktos of AhamMra?

liable, but ought not to be, is one called Dakshi^a-bandha, bondage arising from having to offer gifts to priests, which seems to be condemned as superstitious and mischievous I, As springing from the great mass of philosophic thought accumulated in the Upanishads, the Samkhya, like the Vedanta-philosophy, was probably at first considered as neither orthodox nor unorthodox. It was simply one out of many attempts to solve the riddle of the world, and even the fact that it did not appeal to a personal Lord or creator, was evidently at first not considered sufficient to anathematise it as unorthodox or un-Vedic. It was probably at a much later time when the Vedanta and other systems had already entrenched themselves behind revelation, or the Veda, as the highest authority even on philosophical questions, that other systems, having been proved unVedic, came to be considered as objectionable or unorthodox, while the Vedanta, as its very name implied, was safe under the shadow of the Veda. I know that other scholars maintain that with the S&mkhya any appeal to the Veda was an afterthought only, and not an essential part of the original system, nay, not even quite honest. We may admit that the Samkhya has no need of the Veda, but why should it appeal to it even on indifferent questions, if the Veda had not been considered by it as of supreme authority. It is possible that there may have been originally aA difference between $ruti, revelation as not human, and Aptava&ana, authoritative tradition as human, and that with Kapila the Veda was treated at first as coming under <Aptava&ana. But however this may be, unless our conception of the development of Indian philosophy, as we catch glimpses of it now and then in the course of centuries, is entirely wrong, it must" be clear that, in the present state of our knowledge, to call one channel of philosophic thought, whether Samkhya or Vedanta, in the form in which it has reached us, more ancient than the other, would be mere playing with words.
1 See Tafctya-sam&sa 22 ; Sawkhya-karikas 44.
PARALLEL DEVELOPMENT OP PHILOSOPHICAL SYSTEMS, 235 Parallel development of PMlosopMcal Systems.

The result of this desire to fix dates, where dates are impossible, has often proved most mischievous. Scholars of recognised authority have arrived at and given expression to convictions, not only widely different, but diametrically opposed to each other. The chief cause of this confusion has been that, by a very natural tendency, we always wish to arrange things Nacheinander or in causal connection, instead of being satisfied with taking things as Nebeneinander, parallel and formed under similar conditions, springing from a common source and flowing on side by side in the same direction.

A reference to the history of language may make my meaning clearer. No one would say that Greek was older than Latin. Greek has some forms more primitive than Latin, but Latin also has some forms more primitive than Greek. It is true that we know literary productions in Greek at a much earlier time than literary productions in Latin, nor would any Sanskrit scholar deny that the Sutras of BadarayaTia are older than the Sarakhya-Sfttras, as we now possess the two. But for all that, Greek, as a language, cannot be a day older than Latin. Both branched off, slowly it may be and almost imperceptibly at first, from the time when the Aryan separation took place. In their embryonic form they both go back to some indefinite date, far beyond the limits of any chronology. In India we may learn how, like language, religion, and mythology, philosophy also formed at first a kind of common property. We meet with philosophical ideas of a Yedantic character, though as yet in a very undecided form, as far back as the hymns of the Rig-veda ; they meet us again in the Brahma^as and in some of the Upanishads, while the S&mkhya ideas stand out less prominently, owing, it would seem, to the ascendency gained at that early period already by the Vedanta. Instead of supposing, however, that passages in support of Samkhya ideas occurring in certain of the older Upanishads were foisted in at a later time, it seems far more probable to me that they were* survivals of an earlier period of as yet undifferentiated philosophical thought.
liable, but ought not to be, is one called Dakshma-bandha} bondage arising from having to offer gifts to priests, which seems to be condemned as superstitious and mischievous l.

As springing from the great mass of philosophic thought accumulated in the Upanishads, the Samkhya, like the Vedanta-philosophy, was probably at first considered as neither orthodox nor unorthodox. It was simply one out of many attempts to solve the riddle of the world, and even the fact that it did not appeal to a personal Lord or creator, was evidently at first not considered sufficient to anathematise it as unorthodox or un-Vedic. It was probably at a much later time when the Vedanta and other systems had already entrenched themselves behind revelation, or the Veda, as the highest authority even on philosophical questions, that other systems, having been proved unVedic, came to be considered as objectionable or unorthodox, while the Ved&nta, as its very name implied, was safe imder the shadow of the Veda. I know that other scholars maintain that with the Samkhya any appeal to the Veda was an afterthought only, and not an essential part of the original system, nay, not even quite honest. We may admit that the Samkhya has no need of the Veda, but why should it appeal to it even on indifferent questions, if the Veda had not been considered by it as of supreme authority. It is possible that there may have been originally aA difference between /Sruti, revelation as not human, and Aptava&ana, authoritative tradition as human, and that with Kapila the Veda was treated at first as coming under <lptava&ana. But however this may be, unless our conception of the development of Indian philosophy, as we catch glimpses of it now and then in the course of centuries, is entirely wrong, it must' be clear that, in the present state of our knowledge, to call one channel of philosophic thought, whether Samkhya or Veclanta, in the form in which it has reached us, more ancient than the other, would be mere playing with words.
1 See Tattva-samasa 22 ; Sawkhya-fcarikas 44.
PARALLEL DEVELOPMENT OF PHILOSOPHICAL SYSTEMS. 235 Parallel development of PMlosopfcical Systems.

The result of this desire to fix dates, where dates are impossible, has often proved most mischievous. Scholars of recognised authority have arrived at and given expression to convictions, not only widely different, but diametrically opposed to each other. The chief cause of this confusion has been that, by a very natural tendency, we always wish to arrange things Nacheinander or in causal connection, instead of being satisfied with taking things as Nebeneinander, parallel and formed under simSar conditions, springing from a common source and flowing on side by side in the same direction.

A reference to the history of language may make my meaning clearer. No one would say that Greek was older than Latin. Greek has some forms more primitive than Latin, but Latin also has some forms more primitive than Greek. It is true that we know literary productions in Greek at a much earlier time than literary productions in Latin, nor would any Sanskrit scholar deny that the Sutras of Badarayam are older than the Samkhya-Siitras, as we now possess the two. But for all that, Greek, as a language, cannot be a day older than Latin. Both branched off, slowly it may be and almost imperceptibly at first, from the time when the Aryan separation took place. In their embryonic form they both go back to some indefinite date, far beyond the limits of any chronology. In India we may learn how, like language, religion, and mythology, philosophy also formed at first a kind of common property. We meet with philosophical ideas of a Yedantic character, though as yet in a very undecided form, as far back as the hymns of the Rig-veda; they meet us again in the Brahmams and in some of the Upanishads, while the Samkhya ideas stand out less prominently, owing, it would seem, to the ascendency gained at that early period already by the Ved&nta. Instead of supposing, however, that passages in support of S&mkhya ideas occurring in certain of the older Upanishads were foisted in at a later time, it seems far more probable to me that they were* survivals of an earlier period of as yet undifferentiated philosophical thought.
Buddhism subsequent to TTpanisliads.

What remains of the chronological framework of Indian philosophy is in the end not much more than that both Vedanta and Samkhya ideas existed before the rise of historical Buddhism. The very name of Upanishad, for instance, is so peculiar that its occurrence in ancient Buddhist texts proves once for all the existence of some of these works before the rise of Buddhism.

The recognition of mendicant friars also, as a social institution, seems to me simply taken over from the Brahmans. The very name of Bhikkhu, applied to the members of the Buddhist fraternity, comes from the same source. It is true, no doubt, that the name of Bhikshu does not occur in the classical Upanishads, but the right of begging, whether in the first or the third of the Jlsramas (Brahma/carin or Vanaprastha), is fully recognised, only that the third and fourth Asramas are not so clearly distinguished in early times as they are in Manu and afterwards. In the Kaush. Up. II, 2 we read of a man who has begged through a village and got nothing (Bhikshitva) ; in the Kh&ud. Up. IV, 3, 5, a Brahma&arin is mentioned who has begged. The technical term for this begging is Bhikshafearya in the Br£h. Ar. Up. Ill (V), 5, i, and exactly the same compound, Bhikkhafc&rya, occurs in the Dhammapada 392 ; Bhaiksha&arya occurs also in the MuTicZaka I, 2, n, so that the fact that the substantive Bhikshu does not occur in the classical Upanishads can hardly be used as an argument to prove that the status of the mendicant friar was not known before the spreading of Buddhism. It is true that in its social meaning Aarama, the name of the three or four stages, does not occur in the classical Upanishads ; but, as we find Asramin in the Maitray. Up. IV, 3, we can hardly doubt that the three or four stages (Brahma&ari, GahaW&o, Vanapaft/io, Bhikkhu) were known before the rise of Buddhism, and taken over by the Buddhists from the Vedic Brahmans. Socially, the only Asramas that remained among the Buddhists were two, that of the Gn'hins and that of the That many of the technical terms of the Buddhists (Uposhadha, &c.) could have come from the same source ASVAGHQSHAS BUDDHA-JTARITA. 237 only, has long been known, so much so that it has been rightly said, Without Br^hmanism no Buddhism.

The institution of the Vasso l, for instance, the retreat during the rainy season, is clearly taken over from the Varshas, the rainy season, as kept by the Br&hmans, and so is the quinquennial celebration of the Pa/7/i-avarsha-parishad, and many other customs adopted by the Buddhists.
zh: 這是一項重要的工作，因為我們發現類似的簡短而古老的經文著作，例如《薩爾瓦努克拉瑪》（Sarvanukrama）及其他《努克拉瑪尼》（Anukramanis）著作，我在《古代梵文文獻史》中已有記載。然而，在這類問題上，我們必須避免過度武斷地否定或主張梵文典籍的年代與真實性。我所能說的是，其語言中沒有現代的痕跡，儘管註釋無疑是後出的。使我信服的是一個事實：印度班智達顯然將《本質概論經》（Tattva-samāsa-Sutras）視為數論哲學（Samkhya-philosophy）的原始綱要，而它們乃是後來僞造產物的觀念，就目前我所見，完全缺乏任何真實的論證依據。
吠檀多或梵我一如說的優先性

從所有這些論述來看，以我們目前所掌握的有限手段，要試圖證明《韋丹多》(Vedanta)或《數論》(Samkhya)作為哲學體系的優先性——並將其與我們所據有的《經文》(Sutras)區別開來——是多麼徒勞無功的事。我們沒有外部或歷史證據，而內部證據雖然可能支持某些推測，卻很少能構成確定的證明。我們可以理解，散落在《奧義書》中的種子，如何在時間推移中逐漸發展，最終形成系統的整理與表述，就如同傳留至今、載錄於《韋丹多經文》、《數論》及其他學派經文中的各個體系一樣。不可否認，在《奧義書》時期，韋丹多思想確實比數論思想更為流行。我甚至更進一步，承認《數論哲學》可能是對《不二韋丹多》(Advaita Vedanta)極端一元論的某種緩和。我認為我們能夠理解那些被《韋丹多》——至少按照由商羯羅(Samkara)所代表而非創立的學派的詮釋——不屈不撓的一元論所震驚的人們的顧慮與恐懼。現在，最有可能引發困難的兩個要點是¹ ¹ 這些《阿奴克拉瑪》(Anukramas)已由麥克唐奈爾教授(Professor Macdonell)以極大的謹慎在《牛津逸聞集》(Anecdota Oxoniensia)中出版，我曾將我的資料轉交給他。

或者說，對於普通人的良心而言，最具冒犯性的似乎是對客觀世界實在性的徹底否定，以及主體（亦即我們自身）被要求放棄一切個性。這些觀點即便對我們自身也顯得極其駭人，頗有可能正是這些令人震驚的教義促成了另一個體系的產生，例如我們在數論派（Samkhya）中所發現的體系，便得以擺脫這些駭人之論。這些觀點肯定成為了羅摩努迦（Rāmānuja）以及在他之前的思想家（如博達亞那〔Bodhayana〕和其他《前吠陀》（Pūrva-āryās）的倡導者）的主要絆腳石，驅使他們提出了自己更具人文主義的吠檀多（Vedānta）詮釋，儘管為了維護每一個自我（Purusha）的實在性而犧牲了最高自在天（Īśvara）。

這些關於世界、靈魂與上帝的相互衝突的見解，已經在《奧義書》中浮現；在其中若干篇章——例如《白色雅茱吠陀奧義書》（Śvetāśvatara）、《邁特雷耶奧義書》（Maitray.）與《迦他奧義書》（Kaṭha）——裡頭有一些說法極其接近於我們所知的數論派（Sāṃkhya）學說，而非吠檀多派的教義。然而，吠檀多的觀念在《奧義書》文獻中壓倒性地占據優勢，以至於我們完全可以理解，在各派學校的口頭傳統中，數論派教義應當只發揮了有限的影響，無論它對那些被一元論吠檀多極端見解所排斥的人們享有何等青睞。迦比羅的追隨者相比吠檀多派人士擁有一種優勢，即他們承認存在一個原質（Prakriti），或某種客體的東西，獨立於梵天（Brahman）或神我（Purusha）之外，儘管其被喚入生命與活動只藉由神我的凝視而已，且當該凝視消歇時亦隨之消滅。他們在承認個別靈魂的實在性方面，也較少與人類的共同意識相悖。二元論總是比嚴格的一元論更受歡迎，而數論派在它不僅將自然界視為無明（Avidya）或幻象（Maya）的結果，而且視為常常意義上的某種實在物時，以及當它也賦予個別靈魂或有情物（jīvas）獨立的性質時，顯然是二元論的。應當記得，對於伊濕瓦羅（Isvara）或人格至尊的否定，大概並未構成原初數論派的組成部分，如它在《要義總集》（Tattva-samāsa）中呈現給我們的那樣。因此看來，在這些極其重要的諸點上，數論派更為……

較之於吠檀多而言，這種思想更具調和性，對人類常識的挑戰也較不激烈。雖然這遠非足以證明它在時間上晚於最嚴格形式的吠檀多，但卻足以表明這兩股思想之流乃平行發展，源自共同的古老吠陀思想基金，其後才各自分歧──吠檀多堅定不移地沿著直線前行，而數論則避開了對普通思想家似乎危險的某些思想漩渦。對於普遍民眾而言，自然會認為吠檀多教導的是一切個別靈魂或主體在梵的統一性，以及一切客體事物的幻象性；而數論則至少容許了客體世界的暫時實在性和個別靈魂的多元性。當然，我們目前必須將以下問題視為懸而未決：極端一元論的吠陀觀是否源自商羯羅，抑或如同羅摩努闍一樣，他也能在其對《婆達羅耶那經》(Badaraya^a's Sutras) 的詮釋中援引先阿闍黎們的權威。若果如此，則兩個體系之間的差異確實會顯得不可調和，而其間的次要差異在印度至少可以得到友善的調整。
無神論與正統信仰

即使在每一種哲學中似乎對我們而言都極為重要的一點——有神論或無神論——印度哲學家似乎也能夠達成共識與妥協。我們必須記住，在婆羅門人的眼中，數論派（Samkhya）是無神論的，然而卻是正統的。這對我們來說似乎不可能；但事實是，「正統性」在印度的含義與在我們這裡的含義大不相同。我們所謂的正統性對他們來說不過就是對吠陀最高權威的承認。數論派，無論我們如何評判其吠陀特性，從未直言否定吠陀的權威，儘管它對吠陀的服從可能不甚堅決。數論派在其起源上是否完全獨立於吠陀，實屬難以判斷。某些學者認為對最高權威的承認
對於迦毘羅而言，《聲聞》（Sruti）的地位是事後才有的想法，不過是神學外交手段的產物。但如果果真如此，我們就被迫承認，數論派哲學家希望藉由這種外交手段，將自己提升到與其他人士（例如吠檀多派）之前已經佔據的相同地位；在這個意義上，這似乎可能指示數論作為一套哲學體系的後出性。

重要的是要記得，數論派（Samkhya）不僅宣稱尊奉《吠陀》的權威，而且從未像婆羅門祭祀之神（Brihaspati）或佛陀那樣公然否棄它。至於它是否真正貫徹《吠陀》的精神，特別是《奧義書》的精神，那完全是另一個問題。偉大的吠檀多主義（Vedantism）辯護者商羯羅（Śaṃkara）否認迦毘羅（Kapila）所採納的《吠陀》解釋之正確性，這最多只證明兩人之間存在意見分歧，但同時也表明迦毘羅如同商羯羅一樣，都試圖將自己的哲學表現為得到《吠陀》經文支持的。從《數論頌》（Samkhya-karikas）開篇的一段文句來看，似乎可能迦毘羅將自己的哲學置於《吠陀》之上。但他在那裡實際上所說的只是：《吠陀》所規定的某些除痛救濟之法是好的，而哲學所規定的其他救濟之法同樣也是好的；但兩者之中，後者更好，也就是說更有效力（《根本智論》[Tattva Kaumudi]，v. 2）。這並不影響《吠陀》整體作為與哲學或人類知識相比較之權威地位。我們不應忘記，畢竟是《聲聞》（Śruti）或啟示本身宣告，所有的救濟之法都只不過是舒緩性的，真正的自由（解脫[Moksha]）解脫於一切苦難，只能從哲學知識而來，而這不可比地高於祭祀或其他功德行為（《數論頌義疏》[Samkhya-pravacana]，I，5）。

吠陀在印度宗教史上佔據獨特的地位。它們不僅被視為人類最古老的宗教文獻，更被印度教徒尊為**śruti**（聞慧），即「所聞之物」——直接來自神聖啟示的言詞。這種權威性地位使吠陀超越了單純的歷史文獻，成為印度精神傳統的永恆基礎。 吠陀的權威性源於其被認定為非人作（apauruṣeya），即非人類所作，而是由至高智慧所直接顯現。這一信念使得吠陀經文的每一個音節、每一個詞彙都被賦予了不可侵犯的神聖性。對於印度傳統學者而言，吠陀代表著絕對的真理標準，是所有後世宗教經典與哲學思辨的終極量度。 此種權威性並非源自任何人格化的立法者或先知的宣告，反倒是內在於其本身的神聖言語（mantra）之中。因此，無論是婆羅門教、印度教，乃至稍後演變而來的各種宗派，都必然承認吠陀作為其宗教真理的最高來源。這使吠陀成為比較神話學與宗教學研究中一份無可替代的原始資料，從中我們可以追溯雅利安宗教思想的最早萌芽與演進軌跡。

卡皮拉對吠陀所賦予的權威性，可從他關於知識之三個可能來源所說的話推斷出來，這三個來源是：知覺、推論，以及阿普塔瓦切那（Aptavaçana），即受領的、正確的或真實的言說，或者可以說，是值得信賴之人的言說。他在第五經中以阿普塔斯魯提（Aptasruti）來闡釋阿普塔瓦切那（Aptavaiçana），這清楚地意味著受領的啟示或來自值得信賴來源的啟示。不論注疏家們如何不同意見，斯魯提（śruti）在此只能指吠陀，儘管無疑是卡皮拉所詮釋的吠陀。而且，吠陀不僅被視為等同於感官知覺與推論，更被他置於一個更崇高的地位，這由以下事實所證明：卡皮拉在《經文》第五卷第51則宣稱吠陀是自明的（Svataḥpramāṇam），而知覺與推論則不然，反而被承認易於錯誤，並需要確證。

雖然確實對真正的數論（Samkhya）哲學家而言，《吠陀》並不具有跋陀羅衍那（Badarayam）所賦予的超越人類的權威性，但我無法說服自己相信卡皮拉（Kapila）這一讓步純粹是為了逃脫例如降臨到佛陀身上的命運之詭計。許多段落中，卡皮拉相當自然地訴諸於*Śruti*（啟示）。在《數論經》I, 36中，他訴諸於*Śruti*和尼耶亞（Nyaya）論理二者，但在許多地方他僅訴諸於*Śruti*。啟示應被視為優於經驗或感官知覺，他在I, 147中以白紙黑字陳述此事，其中我們讀到「*Śruti*所確立之事無可否認」。又，當尼耶亞哲學試圖以論理證明感覺器官由元素組成時，卡皮拉以一個簡單的*Śruti*訴求就摧毀了整個論證。他說「它們不可能如此組成，因為*Śruti*說它們是由阿罕喀拉（Ahamkara）即自我意識組成的（II, 30）」。卡皮拉或據評論者認為卡皮拉以*Śruti*的權威為最高的其他段落，可見於《數論經》I, 36；77；83；147；154；II, 50；22；III, 15；80；IV, 22等。
數論派（Sâm3Lhya）敵視祭司階級。
在迦毘羅的經文中，只有一處可以發現對婆羅門祭司階級明顯敵意的痕跡，這段文字似乎蘊含深意。在人類受縛於各種不同形式的束縛之中， --- **注**：原文似乎在此處中斷，最後一句「But are not the elements mere Viktos of AhamMra?」（但這些要素豈非僅為阿漢姆羅的維克多斯？）的上下文脈絡不完整。若有完整原文，請提供以便完整翻譯。

必須被追究責任，但本不應該被追究的，就是所謂的達克希那束縛（Dakshi^a-bandha），即因必須向祭司獻禮而產生的束縛，這似乎被譴責為迷信且有害。 如同從積累在奧義書中的偉大哲學思想泉源而出，三數論（Samkhya）如同韋丹塔哲學一樣，最初可能被認為既非正統亦非非正統。它不過是眾多解決世界謎題之嘗試中的一個，而且即便它並未訴諸個人的主宰者或造物主這一事實，顯然最初也不足以將其貶為非正統或非吠陀的。很可能到了遠後來的時期，當韋丹塔和其他學派已經將自己防守在啟示或吠陀之後，把吠陀視為即使在哲學問題上也是最高權威之時，其他學派既已被證明為非吠陀，便開始被視為有問題或非正統的，而韋丹塔由於其本身的名號所暗示的，則安全地處於吠陀的庇護之下。我知道其他學者主張，對於三數論而言，對吠陀的任何訴諸乃是事後的想法，並非原始體系的本質組成部分，甚至根本稱不上完全誠實。我們可以承認三數論無需吠陀，但若吠陀不曾被其視為至高的權威，它為何要在無關重要的問題上也訴諸於吠陀呢？有可能原來在śruti（啟示，非人為的）與Aptavacana（權威傳統，人為的）之間存有區別，而對於迦毗羅（Kapila）而言，吠陀最初是被視為屬於Aptavacana。但不論如何，除非我們對印度哲學發展的認識（我們在若干世紀的進程中時而得以窺見）全然誤謬，否則必須明確的是，以我們當前的知識狀態而言，說某一條哲學思想的管道，無論是三數論還是韋丹塔，就其已傳遞至我們的形式而言，比另一個更為古老，無異於咬文嚼字。
見《塔迪亞三昧娑》(Tafctya-samḥsa) 22；《數論頌》(Sawkhya-karikas) 44。
哲學體系的平行發展。

這種在不可能確定日期之處硬要確定日期的願望，其結果往往證明是極為有害的。具有公認權威的學者們已經得出並表達了他們的信念，這些信念不僅彼此大相徑庭，而且互相對立。這種混亂的主要原因在於，由於一種非常自然的傾向，我們總是希望按照 *Nacheinander*（時序相繼）的方式或因果聯繫來安排事物，而不願意滿足於將事物視為 *Nebeneinander*（並列相存）——即平行存在、在相似條件下形成、源出於一個共同的源頭，並沿著同一方向並肩流動。

對語言歷史的參考或許能讓我的意思更加明白。沒有人會說希臘語比拉丁語更古老。希臘語確實保存了某些比拉丁語更原始的語法形式，但拉丁語也保存了某些比希臘語更原始的語法形式。誠然，我們所掌握的希臘文文獻產物的時代遠早於拉丁文文獻產物的時代，任何梵文學者也不會否認《婆陀羅闍耶經》（Sutras of Badarayana）比《數論經》（Sarakhya-Sutras）更古老，就我們現今所擁有的兩者而言。然而儘管如此，希臘語作為一門語言，不可能比拉丁語古老哪怕一天。兩者都是在雅利安語族分化之時緩慢地、幾乎難以察覺地從共同源頭分化出來的。在其萌芽形態上，兩者都回溯到某個不確定的時期，遠超越任何年代學的限制。在印度，我們可以學到：如同語言、宗教與神話一樣，哲學最初也形成了一種共同的財產。我們在《梨俱吠陀》的頌歌中遇見具有吠檀多特徵的哲學觀念，儘管當時形式仍十分未定型；這些觀念在《梵書》及某些《奧義書》中再度出現，而《數論》的觀念則顯得不那麼突出，這似乎是因為吠檀多在那個早期時代已經獲得了優勢地位。然而，與其假定在某些較古老的《奧義書》中出現的支持《數論》觀念的段落乃是後世造偽插入的，倒不如說它們更有可能是早期尚未分化的哲學思想的遺留物。
的束縛，但本不應如此，稱為 Dakshma-bandha（祭祀費用之束縛）｝必須向祭司獻禮而產生的束縛，這似乎被譴責為迷信且有害的事¹。

作為源自奧義書中累積的龐大哲學思想體系的分支，數論派（Samkhya）如同吠檀多哲學一樣，在最初很可能既未被視為正統亦未被視為非正統。它不過是眾多解決世界謎題的嘗試之一，而且即便它並不訴諸於個人的主宰或造物主這一事實，在初期顯然也未被認為足以將其貶為非正統或違背吠陀。只有到了相當後來的時期，當吠檀多及其他體系已經將自身牢牢置於啟示，或說吠陀的陰影之下，並視其為最高權威，甚至在哲學問題上亦然之時，其他體系才因被證明為違背吠陀而開始被視為不當或非正統，而吠檀多則因其名稱本身的含義，安全地處於吠陀的庇護之下。我知道其他學者主張，數論派對吠陀的任何訴求不過是事後補充（afterthought），並非原始體系的本質部分，甚至根本不夠誠實。我們可以承認數論派無需吠陀，但如果吠陀未被數論派視為至高無上的權威，為何它要在無關緊要的問題上也訴諸吠陀呢？可能在原始的狀態中，聖聞（Śruti，非人為的啟示）與傳統依據（Āptavacana，人為的權威傳統）之間確實存在著差異，而在迦毗羅那裡，吠陀最初是被當作歸屬於傳統依據之下。但不論如何，除非我們對印度哲學發展的認識——我們在漫長的世紀中時或偶得窺其一二——根本上是錯誤的，否則必須明白，在目前的知識狀況下，稱某一哲學思想流派，無論是數論派或吠檀多，以我們所接觸到的形式而言，比其他體系更古老，不過是在玩弄文字遊戲罷了。
見《本質摘要》（Tattva-samasa）22；《數論頌》（Sawkhya-fcarikas）44。
哲學體系的平行發展。

這種在不可能確定日期之處執意確定日期的欲望，其結果往往造成極大的損害。有公認權威的學者們得出了許多觀點，並對其表達了確信，這些觀點不僅相差懸殊，且往往彼此相反。這一混亂的主要原因在於，出於一種極為自然的傾向，我們總是希望將事物安排成先後相續（Nacheinander）或因果相連的方式，而不願滿足於將事物視為並列（Nebeneinander）、平行發展的，即在相似的條件下形成、源於共同根源、並肩流向同一方向的現象。

參考語言史或許能使我的意思更清楚。沒有人會說希臘語比拉丁語更古老。希臘語確有某些形式比拉丁語更原始，但拉丁語也有某些形式比希臘語更原始。誠然，我們知道希臘文獻的出現時間遠早於拉丁文獻，任何梵文學者也不會否認巴達拉亞那（Badarayam）的經書（Sutra）比數論經書（Samkhya-Sutra）更古老，就我們現今所擁有的兩者而言。但儘管如此，希臘語作為一種語言，不可能比拉丁語早一日。兩者都是在雅利安民族分化的時期，緩慢地、幾乎在最初完全難以察覺的情況下，從共同的源頭分化出來的。在其胚胎形式上，兩者都可以追溯到某個不確定的遠古時代，遠超出任何年代學的界限之外。在印度，我們可以看到語言、宗教和神話一樣，哲學最初也形成了一種共同的產業。我們在梨俱吠陀（Rig-veda）的讚歌中就已經遇到了具有韋丹多（Vedantic）特質的哲學思想，儘管其形式還非常不確定；這些思想在婆羅門書（Brahmanas）和某些奧義書（Upanishads）中再度出現，而數論（Samkhya）的思想則較不突出，其原因似乎是韋丹多已經在那個早期就已取得了優勢。與其假設某些較古老奧義書中支持數論思想的段落是在後世才被篡入的，倒不如說它們更有可能是一個較早時期的遺跡，當時哲學思想尚未分化。
奧義書之後的佛教。
印度哲學編年框架的遺存，最終不過就是吠檀多與數論思想都存在於歷史佛教興起之前這一點。舉例來說，「奧義書」(Upanishad) 這個名稱本身就極為獨特，它在古代佛教典籍中的出現，足以一勞永逸地證明其中某些著作確實存在於佛教興起之前。

對於托缽僧侶（mendicant friars）作為一種社會制度的認可，在我看來，這純粹是從婆羅門傳承而來。應用於佛教教團成員的「比丘」（Bhikkhu）這個名稱本身就出自同一來源。誠然，「比丘」（Bhikshu）這個名稱在古典奧義書中並未出現，但無論在第一或第三個人生階段（梵行期或林棲期；Brahmaçarin 或 Vanaprastha）中，乞討的權利都已獲得充分認可，只是在早期時代，第三和第四人生階段的區分不如後來《摩奴法典》（Manu）及之後的著作中那樣明確。在《考什多基奧義書》（Kaush. Up.）II, 2 中，我們讀到有人在村落中乞討而一無所獲（Bhikshitva）；在《恰多基奧義書》（Kh&ud. Up.）IV, 3, 5 中，提到了一位曾經乞討過的梵行者。這種乞討行為的技術術語在《婆羅門書奧義書》（Br£h. Ar. Up.）III (V), 5, i 中是「Bhiksha&arya」，而完全相同的複合詞「Bhikkha&arya」也出現在《法句經》（Dhammapada）392 中；「Bhaiksha&arya」也見於《蒙德卡奧義書》（MuTicZaka）I, 2, n，因此，僅憑實詞「比丘」（Bhikshu）在古典奧義書中並未出現，這一事實來論證托缽僧侶的身分在佛教傳播之前就不為人知，這種論證很難站得住腳。誠然，在其社會含義上，人生階段（Asrama）這個名稱在古典奧義書中並未出現；但既然我們在《邁特瑞奧義書》（Maitray. Up.）IV, 3 中發現了「Asramin」，我們幾乎不能懷疑這三或四個階段（梵行期、居家期、林棲期、托缽期；Brahma&ari、Gaha&o、Vanapan/io、Bhikkhu）在佛教興起之前就已為人所知，並由佛教徒從吠陀婆羅門那裡傳承而來。在社會層面上，在佛教徒中僅存的人生階段是兩個：居家者（Gn'hins）的階段和托缽者的階段。佛教徒的許多技術術語（如齋戒日；Uposhadha 等）很可能都出自同一來源，這一點早已為人所知，因此有句話說得當之無愧：「無婆羅門教，則無佛教。」
例如說，瓦索爾（the Vasso l）的制度——即雨季期間的禁足——顯然是從婆羅門所遵守的雨季（Varshas）沿襲而來的，佛教徒所採納的五年一度的巴利雨季大會（Pa/7/i-avarsha-parishad）以及許多其他風俗亦然。
